EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On Tuesday, June 18th, 2013 at 330 pm PDT an EF0 tornado with winds between 65 and 85 mph occurred 4 miles northwest of Roseburg in Douglas County, Oregon. This is the first documented tornado to occur in Douglas County based on the Storm Prediction Center���s (SPC) database going back to 1950.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A neighbor witnessed the tornado for about 1 minute. She heard a very loud noise and looked out the window where she saw ���a funnel cloud on the ground��� whipping around very fast and blowing apart a carport canopy sheltering a boat. The carport canopy was held down by four-gallon sized buckets filled with water. These buckets went flying into the boat and fence. The boat received a gash, and the metal pipe fence rail was broken. A satellite dish on the roof was also turned 90 degrees. The tornado then moved into an empty field and dissipated. There was no damage to the house, but the Christmas lights were pulled down.

The Sunnyside Turnoff Fire is 51,340 acres. Despite continuing gusty winds and hot temperatures yesterday, the fire remained within containment lines and had no perimeter growth. The fire is located on lands of the Confederated Tribes of Warm Springs (CTWS) east of U.S. Highway 26 near Warm Springs, Oregon. ||Overall containment has reached 95%. Specifically the fire remains south of the S-300/330 road; east of the S-110 road along the western perimeter; northeast of Upper Dry Creek road; north and west of the Deschutes River; and south of Antoken Creek along the northeast flank. ||Mop-up and rehabilitation of firelines no longer needed for overall containment will continue today. In addition, all divisions will be patrolled. ||Total resources assigned to the fire has decreased to 499. Many of those being released are re-assigned to other emerging fire suppression priorities in other parts of Oregon and Washington. ||Oregon Interagency Incident Management Team 4 (OR IIMT 4) will turn back management of the Sunnyside Turnoff Fire to a local Incident Management Team (Incident Commander Herkshan) tonight at 8 p.m..
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Final update issued by incident commander.

A combination of strong pressure gradients and convective outflow from a squall line thunderstorm produced damaging straight line winds on March 20th.  Pendleton ASOS reported a 61mph gust with the gust front.  The Umatilla county sheriff reported power outages and large trees uprooted in the downtown area.  About 15 to 20 minutes later a pump house was destroyed and trees were uprooted in Milton-Freewater.  The top portion of a 50 foot tree snapped in half and was across highway 11.  CWS Orion with a microserver weather instrument reported a 76 MPH wind gust in Milton-Freewater.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Negatively tilted shortwave trough and the cold front associated with it aided in the development of late afternoon thunderstorms across northeast Oregon.  Convection began just east of the Cascades and began to intensify into stronger to severe thunderstorms by the time it reached Morrow county.  3 Miles North of Heppner a pear tree had several limbs broken off and about 5 minutes of high winds.  Pendleton had a downed tree, damage to a home, and power lines down, and a barn destroyed from the high winds.  7 miles north of Helix a mesonet measured a gust of 74 MPH.  Over 2800 people were without power in the city of Weston due to the high winds from the severe thunderstorm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Pear tree had several limbs broken off.

A low pressure center, approximately 1000 millibars moved over Southwest Washington near Gray's Harbor, Washington and produced Strong winds for the Oregon Coast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained winds of 44 mph was measured at the NOS Garibaldi (TLB03) station with gusts up to 59 mph. Similar winds were measured for the Megler Bridge on US 101 (ODT76), Clatsop Spit (3CLO3), and Pacific City (AT297). The peak gust at Pacific City was 65 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system moved over the area during a period of cold weather. The preceding cold temperatures were due high pressure that extended from Central Canada to just east of the Cascades. East winds ushered the very cold air through the Columbia River Gorge and throughout most of Northwest Oregon. Moisture from this low resulted in heavy snow for many parts of the Central and Southern Willamette Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: NWS Spotters, Emergency Managers, the media and the general public reported 4 to 9 inches of snow in the South Willamette Valley. The highest amounts of 8 to 9 inches were measured in Corvallis, Philomath, and Creswell.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Cold air from a strong High East of the Cascades combined with strong east winds resulted in Extremely cold wind chills for the Oregon Cascades.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The weather station at Government Camp (GOV50) reported temperatures between -2 and 2 degrees with sustained winds of 22 mph. The peak gust was 60 mph. These values resulted in -20 to -30 degree Fahrenheit wind chills. The temperature and wind at Tombstone Pass (ODT50) calculated to wind chills of -15 to -25 degrees Fahrenheit.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Temperature and Winds at The Oregon Department of Transportation weather station at Willamette Pass calculated to -20 degrees Fahrenheit. There is uncertainty on the ending time of this event because the wind sensor failed on the 7th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front swept through the Intermountain West early in the morning on the 23rd. Snow squalls and high winds with damage to trees and power lines were reported across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A cold front moving through the region brought high winds up to 69 MPH and wind damage around the Diamond and Mann Lake areas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level jet moved over the area on Sunday April 7th.  Strong winds aloft mixed down and produced high winds across portions of northeast Oregon.  High sustained winds and wind gusts in MPH are listed as followed: 5 miles WSW of Shaniko had (45) sustained, (59) gust 8 WSW Grass Valley, (59) gust 5WNW City of the Dalles, 8 miles WSW of Echo had (41) sustained, and (75) gust at the coal plant in Boardman.
Rip currents along the Oregon Coast resulted in one fatality and multiple rescues near Lincoln City.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 54 year-old Vancouver woman died after being caught in a rip current near Lincoln City. Two other swimmers were able to swim back to shore.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rip currents along the Oregon Coast resulted in one fatality and multiple rescues near Lincoln City.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two people were rescued from the D River Wayside near Lincoln City, Oregon. Shortly after this rescue, three more people were rescued from rip currents at Roads End Park near Lincoln City.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Some nocturnal thunderstorms generated numerous lightning strikes over southwest Oregon in the early morning hours on 07/26/2013. The lightning started dozens of fires, one of which developed into the Brimstone Wildfire.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: As of 08/01/13, this fire covered 1711 acres and was 15 percent contained. Fire fighting expenditures at this point was 4.3 million dollars.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Some nocturnal thunderstorms generated numerous lightning strikes over southwest Oregon in the early morning hours on 07/26/2013.  The lightning started dozens of fires, four of which developed into the Douglas Complex.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This complex consists of four fires.  As of 08/01/13, it covered 28496 acres and was 7 percent contained.  Fire fighting expenditures at this point was 6.0 million dollars.

CZ_NAME: EAST SLOPES OF THE OREGON CASCADES
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Lightning sparked a fire about 14 miles north of Sisters OR on August 1st.  A local news reports that 1 firefighter was killed and 2 were injured while working on the fire.  The fire has grown to roughly 500 acres or 0.78 square miles.  The injuries occurred as the tree fallers worked to suppress the fire.  One firefighter was killed by a falling tree snag while battling the wildfire.
The Riffle Wildfire started on 09/16/2013 in southeast Lake County.  The cause was under investigation.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The last report on the Riffle wildfire stated that it covered 1007 acres and was 90% contained.  Estimated containment date was 09/21/13.  200,000 dollars were spend on firefighting efforts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Lightning started the Vinegar Fire on August 12 about 27 miles NE of John Day Oregon.  The fire would continue into September with an estimated containment day of September 30th.
A logger was injured when lightning struck while he was holding a steel choker cable.   He received a severe shock, but recovered and returned to work four days later.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Roseburg News-Review reported that a logger was injured when lightning struck while he was holding a steel choker cable.   He received a severe shock and was taken to Coquille Valley hospital.  He recovered and returned to work four days later.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Some nocturnal thunderstorms generated numerous lightning strikes over southwest Oregon in the early morning hours on 07/26/2013. The lightning started dozens of fires, two of which developed into the Big Windy Complex Wildfire.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Big Windy wildfire burned all month.  As of the end of August, the fire covered 24253 acres and was 82 percent contained.  33.2 million dollars were spent on firefighting efforts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front combined with instability associated with an upper level low off the coast of California to generate severe convection in parts of Southeast Oregon and Southwest Idaho.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Malheur County Sheriff dispatch reported several large trees and power lines down in Vale, Oregon and power outages in Ontario, Oregon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moist southerly flow across eastern Oregon induced severe convective activity and flash flooding over parts of Harney County, Oregon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported high winds that blew down at least one tree.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unusually cold air mass combined with clearing skies in offshore flow allowed freezing temperatures to develop along the Coos and Douglas county coasts in southern Oregon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reported low temperatures ranged from 23 to 37 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain resulted in flooding of the the Wilson River near Tillamook as well as urban flooding in the Portland metro area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Around 7 inches of rain fell for the North Oregon Coast from September 28, 2013 to September 30, 2013 resulting in the Wilson River near Tillamook to exceed Flood Stage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain resulted in flooding of the the Wilson River near Tillamook as well as urban flooding in the Portland metro area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: KPTV-KPDX Broadcasting reported that heavy rain resulted in flooding and damage to the Legacy Good Samaritan Medical Center and several businesses in Northwest Portland. Besides damage to the hospital's emergency and operating room, some elective surgeries were cancelled.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic air mass brought clearing skies and cold temperatures to southwest Oregon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reported low temperatures ranged from 30 to 36 degrees.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reported low temperatures ranged from 27 to 34 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An historically cold air mass moved into Oregon December 6-7, and this led to low level snows and very cold temperatures.  A Freeze Warning was issued for the coast continuously from 07/0307 PST until 08/1000 PST as the forecast indicated that temperatures in some areas would not rise above freezing during that interval.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reported low temperatures on 12/7 ranged from 29 to 32 degrees.  All reporting stations recorded above freezing highs on 12/7. Reported low temperatures on 12/8 ranged from 26 to 30 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several thunderstorms rolled through the area on Sunday August 25th. Some of which had: heavy rain, blowing dust, strong gusty winds and large hail. The largest hail reported was 1.75 inches and the highest estimated wind speed was about 75 MPH.  We had a fatality due to lightning.  A spotter reported over 2 inches of rainfall in just an hour in Jefferson County at the Crooked River Ranch. Some photos were submitted to the office of the standing water and some of the washed out dirt roads in the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous irrigation wheel lines damaged and quarter sized hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another round of thunderstorms made their way through portions of central and northeast Oregon.  One of the thunderstorms became severe with 1.00 inch hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The first strong system of the season brought high winds to portions of southern Oregon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Onion Mountain RAWS recorded numerous gusts exceeding 58 mph during this interval.  The peak gust was 77 mph, recorded at 29/1845 PST. The Quail Prairie RAWS recorded gusts to 58 mph or greater from 29/1839 to 29/2039 PST, with a peak gust of 60 mph at 29/1939 PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The first strong system of the season brought high winds to portions of southern Oregon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Squaw Peak RAWS recorded numerous gusts exceeding 75 mph during this interval.  The peak gust was 92 mph, recorded at 28/1839 and 28/1939 PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain over the course of about an hour allowed for a mudslide across central Grant County, just south of the John Day area.
A disturbance moved across the Pacific Northwest and produced numerous thunderstorms across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain and hail caused debris to cover part of the access road between Government Camp and the Timberline Lodge. The hail size was estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very weak EF0 tornado briefly touched down 4 miles northeast of Lostine on Thursday May, 9th.  The track was 0.1 miles and had a width of 10 yards.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: COOP observer sent in pictures and a brief video of the short lived tornado.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong convection developed over south central Oregon on this afternoon and evening...and several storms became severe with quarter sized hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A member of the public at Crescent Lake Resort reported quarter sized (1.0 inch) hail with a thunderstorm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A disturbance moved across the Pacific Northwest and produced numerous thunderstorms across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm produced 1.5 inch diameter hail on Highway 26 around 3 miles west of Government Camp.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Flooding occurred at the Umatilla at Gibbon and Pendleton.  The water washed out a section of the road near the Umatilla Forks Campground.  There was pasture and low area flooding, water over the paths in Riverfront part in Hermiston.

A cool area of low pressure moved across Western Oregon producing several funnel clouds and one confirmed tornado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado touched down near the intersection of 14th St. and NE Kirby St. in McMinnville Oregon. Several 8-10 inch diameter trees were snapped at this location. The tornado continued Southeast for one block through a residential neighborhood with some tree damage noted. The tornado lifted a camper and deposited it over a chain link fence in a nearby industrial area and lofted 300 pound trellises. This damage was consistent with EF1 wind speeds. The tornado dissipated as it moved Southeast of 11th Ave and Lafayette. Some straight line wind damage was noted at a nearby park after this time.
